[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight as I write this, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Let's start things out with a quote again: 
 
"Political activities are a terrible temptation to the occultist; knowing what he does, it is very difficult for him to avoid the use of his knowledge and power to amend abuses, and in doing so he is very likely to run ahead of the times and do more harm than good.  It seems as if fanaticism is inseparable from the application of the principles of the higher life to politics, and spiritual zeal has shed quite as much blood as worldly ambition." 
 
That's from Dion Fortune's Practical Occultism in Daily Life, one of the books of hers that did an enormous amount to shape my understanding of magic and the occult.   

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Any question received after then will not get an answer, and will likely just be deleted.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.
 
I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed -- and yes, this means you.  See you next week!***

[IMAGE:1]

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I prefer the classical seven-loop round aka "Minoan" pattern, drawn or posted on or near the interior of the entrance door, of a size to be easily visible from a step or two away from the door. (I'm pretty sure having the pattern on the floor would be better still, but I've never had the opportunity to actually try that.) The material and medium don't matter, though for some reason I prefer a dark green color for the labyrinth pattern, with the path itself blank. If I were doing it today I would put a picture-frame of wood moulding on an exterior wall and fill it in with blackboard paint, and I'd erase and redraw the pattern with chalk each lunar month.

(I'm not doing it these days because my current home's surroundings are etherically healthy.)

The pattern shouldn't be hidden or covered. It's not a talisman; it doesn't do anything at all by itself just being there. You should take note of it daily, focusing on the intention of it forming a barrier to turbulent or disruptive etheric flows. Visualize etheric energy from outside flowing through it but being drawn in and channeled along its path and being quieted and elevated in the process. (Hence, the "filter" analogy.)

When entering your home (or leaving, but that's less necessary if your home itself is etherically clean), pause just inside the door to trace the path. You can do that literally using a finger, or visually, directly on the pattern or using it as a guide; or, with practice, in your mind's eye, visualizing yourself inside the pattern. The idea is to be mentally in the center when you take the last step to, or the first step from, the door (or more practically, just inside the door). From the center, the whole pattern emanates from you and you can will any unwanted etheric crud to dissolve into the pattern. Then trace the path back out in the same way.

The use of the Celtic knot patterns you describe was part of the design concept here, along with a lot of other bits and pieces of labyrinth lore and known fragments of historical usages and placements, and the usual trial and error. (One key meditation question behind it: why all the myths and lore about getting lost in patterns that traditionally have no maze-like branching?) A well-chosen knot (or spiral, etc) might work just as well; someone would have to try and see.

comments.title: Myths
comments.content:
Hi JMG, Darren here. Hoping to pick up on your reply to my question on the other blog.

I asked about the lower mental plane and what are myths? You replied; myths are narratives that we use to interpret the world. The myth of progress was a helpful narrative for a while but no longer produces helpful interpretations of the world we experience and needs to be replaced with different narratives that are more useful.

My response is "oh, so we need to think up new myths".

But in a reply to Jbucks you said:

"any attempt to create or renew myths will have at most a personal effect unless it’s tapping into changes already at work in the higher planes of existence. One of the reasons that I’m encouraging people to think about different myths is precisely that the habit of attending to the mythic will give the higher planes more opportunities to communicate to us the myths we need."

My questions:

1/ So the source of these overarching narratives is the higher planes? Even the myth of progress would have come down through the planes at a time it was needed?

2/ My understanding then is that our role is to open ourselves to different narratives, by attending to the mythic with a mindset of "receiving" - is that right?. To that end I purchased Stephen Fry's retelling of the Greek myths.

3/ Where would we look for the first buds of new myths? It seems to me writers of fiction and screenplays would be likely conduits in our civilisation. Maybe even game developers.

Many thanks

comments.title: Dreams
comments.content:
Hi there,

I've been a big fan for a long time. I've never asked a question before, but I just have to this time.

Over the last few years I've had a number of dreams - true dreams, not gibberish dreams - where entities that seemed very familiar from another place were telling me that I had to die in the next few years. Even included a scene of me chasing after them in their spaceship on my bicycle because I hadn't died on time. I wasn't too worried about dying, but I think my family will need me for more than a few more years, so I was a bit uncomfortable about these dreams.

Then one night I was reading your Merlin book. I fell asleep (sorry). A young guy with light brown reddish hair appeared in my dream and very specifically said he was Merlin Ambrosius. (I was just reading your book.) He kicked me out of my body - I felt myself go - and we both looked at my body, which was now transparent. We could see a tumour on my right ovary. He didn't seem too concerned about me dying either, but he said I had some serious health problems and I shouldn't go to a doctor, but I should start drinking a litre of carrot juice every day and I would get better. He said it was vitally important that I live on in good health, without getting sick. When I asked why, he just said something along the lines of "for the things that will come to pass" or some such. So I'm none the wiser there.

So I have two questions:

(1) First I had to die, now I have to live. Do you have any idea what's going on?
(2) What's the deal with your book?

Thanks for any perspective.

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content:
1) Some people dream true. Most people dream in symbols, and have to grapple with the symbols in meditation and reflection in order to figure out what they mean. I have no way of knowing which you are, thus can't tell what's going on.

2) Not my book, but the One who inspired it and works through the mysteries taught in it. I trust you're following his advice!

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content: Really scared not to, after he specially visited and gave me one job!

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content: Nah, don't be scared. It was a gift, not a job, and will be followed by other gifts if you make good use of it.

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content:

So that supports there being at least something in the air symbolized by carrots, but that doesn't mean it's literal...

I'd hedge my bets, and get second and third medical opinions!

My son named himself one of Merlin's names when I was pregnant, but he still nearly died at birth without the kind of medical attention you can only get from a surgeon, he's not a real wizard ;-)

replies.title: Re: Dreams
replies.content:
Thanks for your concern, but no, no, it's definitely literal. That's what he said. He also specifically said not to go to a doctor.

I have a long history with this stuff, and you don't need to question it, it's the best advice you'll ever get. If a true dream says carrot juice, then it's carrot juice.

And by the way, the carrot juice is working awesomely, better than I ever could have imagined. But that's what I expect from a true dream!

Interesting you're also dreaming of carrots. Carrots have evolved over millions of years to overcome threats, you know.

Obvious enough subject given some of the exercises you recommend, but then wondered about the idea of always handing a knife to someone handle first (previously just thought of a safety and politeness), and not leaving chef's knives on the table pointing at a particular person - not sure where I got that but thought it might be from martial arts.

Anyway, as I thought about this I wondered about the 'idea' of not wanting a very sharp and pointy knife facing in my direction (like never pointing a loaded weapon) and started to explore the thought. What if the discomfort was not within my narratives but something else piggy-backing?

Crazy idea perhaps.
Anyway, I thought to experiment with this and got a nice but ordinary knife with an 11cm" (4.3") blade, set it on the table in front of me with the blade pointing directly at me.
Had a distinctly odd sense of mental discomfort but no point of origin.
Established a state of mind and, focussing on the blade and using visualisation, imagined the blade doing the poking motion.

Wondered if a single instance of the blade could be evaded so set up 'instances' of the blade both behind and to the sides.
The 'poking' movement seems important - visualising the blades just moving around the perimeter did not have the same feeling as the poking motion.

As well as the knife itself, the focus was on a particular energy state (or nature) - the blade was not just a physical tool but had a cleansing energy state as used in working with energy channels.

There's more, but you no doubt get the drift...

One thing I will mention - during the visualised movements of the blade instances, I felt a distinct counter balance mirrored from the inside and pointing out - like the blades on the outside were somehow joined by yet another set within.
Also seemed to trigger other 'exercises' but that might be just 'tracks in space' activating.

Something definitely seemed to be happening to my energy field but no idea if any larvae involved!
The closest I can come to describing what it felt like is it being similar to an exercise that uses a bunch of around hundred fine gauge (2mm) steel rods to stimulate meridians and 'shake stuff loose'.

The process dissolved the sense of mental discomfort and resulted in an after-state like after a cleansing (banishing?) - calm and clear.

Beyond the physical use of objects (like rods mentioned above), I have never previously used an external object like a knife as an adjunct to a cleansing meditation - are there any cautions you suggest to consider while experimenting?

The visualised instances started at around 2' (60cm) then moving inwards and at a certain point this seemed to trigger a cleansing force within... bit like the inward motion of pistons of a combustion engine reached a trigger/pressure that fired-up/initiated a force from within and this thrust force went off on yet another angle.
Having the physical blade present, right there pointing at me seems to make the effect much stronger than pointed away or not present, but I'll need to experiment more.

Absolutely fascinating!
[earthworm]

Do you have any occult advice about how to not be personally affected by memes?

Today's quote is pertinent as I stay away from political magic, because I know I don't have the spare energy for that the way some people might. However, occasionally there are phenomena with a political dimension that I need to try to keep away from myself because of negative effects they'd have on me personally. This, I would say, is one of them.

I am a middle-aged white woman with chronic illness. I have short hair because that's easier to manage when you're ill, and I always preferred it anyway.

I've had very little interaction with service businesses over the last year, but sooner or later I'll have to. There will be things where, because of my illness, I have to make requests to do things differently, which might be irritating for the providers, and which have serious consequences for my health if they don't. I've usually had a good ear for choosing people who are kind and helpful and follow requests, but sometimes things need to be done where I couldn't find there isn't someone like that, and I have to keep on at them about something.

I've learnt and been told over the years that you should just ask directly and politely, you shouldn't always have to explain everything about yourself in detail or present yourself as pathetic and unconfident in order to get people to help. I don't want to have to do all that all the time as it makes me feel worse and increases vulnerability. Invisible disability means I look like "a Karen" but I don't usually look like "a disabled person" (and if I did that would mean things had got a lot worse and I am all about trying to stop that happening).

I am worried that the rise of the Karen meme is going to mean that is the only way, and that even then, because of the meme and the way it might be changing attitudes, I am going to find it harder to get certain needs met safely, because people will feel they are doing the right thing to disregard extra, apparently persnickety, instructions from someone like me, when my life is already getting harder and more stressful.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The problem you face can be framed another way. The Karen meme didn't happen by accident; it was a reaction to a social phenomenon that anyone who's worked in retail, restaurants, or service industries has had to deal with for years. Long before the name "Karen" became a meme, for example, in the days when I worked as a counter clerk in a variety of settings, I learned to dread the arrival of women of the comfortable classes who combined a wildly overinflated sense of entitlement with a habit of abusing their social inferiors -- i.e., me and my coworkers. There were, and still are, a great many such people, and your problem is that you risk being mistaken for one.

One of the first lessons any competent mage learns is that changing yourself is a more effective approach than trying to force other people to change. You've taken an important first step by realizing that the set of behaviors that you've been taught for dealing with service businesses will very likely get you treated as a Karen. The effective magical approach to this is to change your behaviors so that this mistake doesn't happen.

That doesn't mean presenting yourself as pathetic and unconfident. It means recognizing that your special needs place a burden on service employees, and that they don't necessarily have the training they would need to anticipate your requirements and deserve enough explanation to understand your needs. It also means being friendly to them, showing gratitude for the help they give you, and letting them know you realize that your special needs are a burden to them. Oh, and leave a decent tip in all cases and a lavish one when you get good service. That way you won't risk being mistaken for a Karen; instead, you'll be that really nice lady who's always so friendly when she comes in, and you'll get the care and consideration that friendly customers inevitably get.

comments.title: Science, Laws and Planes
comments.content:
One of the standard materialist views on material reality is, that one day we will have a mathematical theory of everything including the origin and fate of our universe. And this of course proves (for unknown reasons, if you think about it), that there can be only material things since there is a theory for it but not (unless you choose to take a closer look) for other than material things.

While that logic is obviously flawed, it made me thinking about the planes of existence beyond physical matter:

1) The case of a mathematical theory and it's application seem to illustrate the up and down on the planes very well. Very roughly sketched: Abstract theory (upper mental), for example quantum electrodynamics, general relativity, etc. -> Framing in concrete mathematical symbols and application of those to a certain phenomenon, e.g. hydrogen atom, planetary system, etc. (lower mental) -> Making sense of this by translating into mental images and subjective analogies (astral) -> Observations, experiments, etc. on the material plane. Of course there is an upward arc to this, coming from observations made with our material senses ranging to abstract theory. The continued up and down leads to more complexity (in knowledge, theories, experimental design, etc.)

2) The argument, "there's a law for everything, so it has to be material!" - I mean, there's a LAW. What is a law? Why is it there, why is it like it is and not different, are there other laws we don't know of? etc. For me, this points towards planes above the mental that are the foundation out of which something like a law can descend, something beyond our logic (which presumably does not mean that it's illogical but that it has a logic we cannot perceive as such). The fact that ordinary humans cannot grasp this and go beyond abstract theory seems to be indicative that we do not have senses above the mental plane (yet). Does that make sense?

3) This in turn points toward the divine, since when you operate on a plane on which you can "design" laws, there's nothing you can't do except what the laws of your plane and those above... good heavens... does it ever stop? Limitations of ordinary logic at work! And pondering your last posts on the CosDoc and some of the comments - wouldn't you even need to work at least two (sub-)planes above the mental in order to effectively shape the mental plane?

4) Many of the better scientists seem to be fairly evolved for certain very special tasks on the mental plane. Which is nice, but translated on the material plane... "Hey that's a super cool arm you have there, but where's the rest of the body?"

I have to say I feel a little stiff thinking in terms of the CosDoc, which may have to to with the fact that I grew up in the world of the mental walking arms (mental, truly), but it's fascinating stuff. How many dismiss this as superstitious nonsense without even noticing the explanatory power of this model not only to "magic" but to existence as a whole... Thanks a lot for your sharing your thoughts on the CosDoc with your audience! I presumably wouldn't have discovered it otherwise.

Cheers,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: help, mind too empty
replies.content:
That's one of the downsides of mind-emptying meditation -- it teaches you not to think. You might try learning the sevenfold wheel of thought. There are seven concepts and seven questions, and you combine them.

Concepts:
Being
Becoming
Source
Substance
Power
Purpose
Value

Questions:
Whether?
What?
How?
Who?
Where?
When?
Why?

You combine each of the questions with each of the concepts, thus...

Whether it is?
What is it?
How is it?
Who (or whose) is it?
Where is it?
When is it?
Why is it?
Whether it becomes?
What does it become?
How does it become?

...and so on. If those forty-nine questions won't give you something to think about, you're not trying hard enough. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)

1) Fortune wrote at a time when going to fortunetellers to try to find out the future was an extremely common habit, and led a lot of people into a purely passive relationship to their own future. She was trying to break people out of that. Fortunately things have changed a bit.

2) The planes are discrete in exactly the same way that, say, visible light and radio waves are discrete and not continuous. They have different effects, follow somewhat different laws, and you can't treat them interchangeably; at the same time, there's a zone where you can call certain wavelengths microwaves or you can call them infrared light, depending on exactly how you want to classify them.

replies.content:
The way I understand the I-Ching, which I presume to be the same for other oracles, is that they do not tell what it is to happen; there would be no point to ask an oracle if the future where fully predestined!

Instead, the first hexagram tells you of your present, what is your situation right now. The second hexagram tells you of the forces (seen or otherwise) that are pushing your life towards this or that direction. From that you can tell where you are likely to land, much like you could do with Newtonian physics. But the analysis only remain certain in the forces themselves do not change, which in general is less likely the longer time into the future you want to project the forecast.

(Unsurprisingly, it is traditionally considered a bad omen to pull the same hexagram twice in a row. The way it was explained to me is, that you are stuck in your current situation, as if your life was put on hold).

And what to do with that information? You can only add so much force into the mix, so it is best to roll with the punches. It's like a surfer that cannot stop the sea, but with skill and strength can keep his own body in balance so to ride the waves.

Disclaimer: I stopped doing the I-Ching years ago, after the oracle itself told me to stop nagging him and to grow confident that, even if unseen, "Mother" is never far from me. In retrospective, I had been using it as a crutch to deal with my own anxieties.

CRPatino

replies.title: Re: An example of magical fanatics
replies.content: I’d say for one they cast too wide a net.

replies.title: Re: An example of magical fanatics
replies.content:
Oh dear gods.

Okay, first of all, Austin Osman Spare's sigil magic, while it can be useful, is not a very powerful magical tool because it draws on nothing more than the energy and imagination of the individual mage. There are powers in the cosmos far greater than anything human; high magic calls on those powers to get its effects. Spare's sigil magic, like most modern pop-culture magic, ignores that and acts as though human beings are the biggest, baddest things in the cosmos -- which we aren't. So she's trying to light up a skyscraper with a single 9 volt battery.

Second, Spare's method is hopelessly vulnerable to a particular kind of counterspell. Anyone with basic magical knowledge can recognize a Spare-style sigil and decompose it into its letters. Once you've done that, you use the same letters to construct a different phrase or sentence with a conflicting intention; you do a series of workings meant to charge the sigil with that conflicting intention; and in a case like this one, you spread the conflicting meaning among everyone on your side of the magical war, so that whenever they see the sticker, they think of the conflicting meaning, and not the original one. The interference pattern set up by the clash of intentions absorbs all the power put into the original working, and it fails. It's quite an elegant counterspell, and I'm far from the only person who knows it.

Third, Mars and Saturn are opposing forces. Mars is hot, dynamic, and brings violent change. Saturn is cold, static, and resists change. Combining the two means that you get neither one. One-pointedness is essential.

Fourth, since she didn't choose a single focus for her work, what energy she's able to put into her sigil and its artificial entities will be diffused over a laundry list of targets, none of which will be significantly affected by them. Here again, one-pointedness is essential!

Fifth, she knows nothing about who and what she's fighting. Sun Tzu wrote, "if you know yourself and know your enemy, in a hundred battles you will have a hundred victories." She thinks she's fighting Dominionist Christians, and that's simply not true -- the populist movement that's coalesced around Trump is a whole different animal, and so her aim is bad. Even if the other points I've made weren't at issue, all she'd do is give some Dominionists a bad time, while Trump and his followers saunter by unharmed.

This last is to my mind the most lethal weakness of the privileged left: they've embraced the delusion that they create their own reality. But a bunny-slope magical technique does not become a powerful spell just because they say it is, any more than a populist uprising against an incompetent and kleptocratic managerial class does not become a Dominionist Christian movement just because they say it is! Reality is what it is, and insisting that it has to cater to your sense of entitlement is a great way to have a miserable life and an early death.

comments.title: Nominalism
comments.content:
A few weeks ago you mentioned the argument that nominalism, originating with William of Ockham in the West, led to a collapse of traditional values.

I've seen this argument in neo-traditionalist circles a lot.

I don't find it very convincing because nominalism features prominently in many schools of Buddhism, especially Mahayana schools which accept Nagarjuna, and yet Buddhism doesn't deny the value of "conventional" truth and karma, even if on the ultimate level, they are said not to have essences -- essences are conventional designations rather than truly existent separately from each instance.

Of course, the entire cultural context behind Western and Buddhist nominalism was different, but there was some speculation that even Hume was influenced by Ippolito Desideri's journals of his mission in Tibet and his understanding of Buddhist philosophy.

Anyway, I am curious what persuaded you of this argument, if you could share.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Nominalism
replies.content: Er, you're misquoting me.  "Traditional values" aren't something I worry about much. Morals, in my view, are human cultural constructs and nothing more; they're valuable -- we need some set of rules governing interpersonal behavior so that we can live together in relative peace -- but many different sets of moral values can do that, and there's no one set of "traditional values" share by all or even most peoples. No, nominalism -- and here I mean specifically Western nominalism, with its distinct cultural context -- ended up playing a large role in laying the foundations of modern rationalist materialism, as in the West -- as distinct from in India and points east -- the rejection of essences led promptly to the rejection of non-material realities across the board. (Buddhism's far more sophisticated epistemology kept the Buddhist nominalists from making so childish a mistake.)

comments.title: Do I stay or do I go now?

I remembered a book I once read about brain development where the researcher said she thought of childhood as the Ideas Department of human life - the time of unfettered creativity and absorption of tools to think. Then as we transform to adults, we become the Engineering Department, where we refine our ideas to working concepts and execute them. We can exercise creativity as adults, but it's harder work for us and constrained to fewer avenues.

Though there are some kids who are acting very differently, depending on how their parents are behaving. The little boy with the paranoid mother had hysterics on the beach today, his father couldn't drop him off, he was screaming he was afraid of the beach, afraid of the other children, afraid of me and the teacher. He had never been like that before, he's the chillest little dude. He did calm after an hour, and he's slowly returning to his normal self, but he won't take his bike helmet off. There was one day last week where my kids acted like that, but they're back to normal. It was a heck of a week, though.

I'd aligned my life, my habits, up the way I wanted it, and I'd just gather data while waiting for the next push. replies.title: Re: Do I stay or do I go now?
replies.content:
Terry Pratchett said--far more seriously than not--that powerful stories have their own lives, quite apart from the people who tell (or live) them. Here's what he wrote in the first pages of one of his novels ("Witches Abroad"):


[S]tories are important.
People think that stories are shaped by people. In fact, it’s the other way around.
Stories exist independently of their players. If you know that, the knowledge is power.
Stories, great flapping ribbons of shaped space-time, have been blowing and uncoiling around the universe since the beginning of time. And they have evolved. The weakest have died and the strongest have survived, and they have grown fat on the retelling . . . stories, twisting and blowing through the darkness.
And their very existence overlays a faint but insistent pattern on the chaos that is history. Stories etch grooves deep enough for people to follow in the same way that water follows certain paths down a mountainside. And every time fresh actors tread the path of the story, the groove runs deeper.
This is called the theory of narrative causality and it means that a story, once started, takes a shape. It picks up all the vibrations of all the other workings of that story that have ever been.
This is why history keeps repeating all the time.
So a thousand heroes have stolen fire from the gods. A thousand wolves have eaten grandmother, a thousand princesses have been kissed. A million unknowing actors have moved, unknowing, through the pathways of story.
It is now impossible for the third and youngest son of any king, if he should embark on a quest which has so far claimed his older brothers, not to succeed.
Stories don’t care who takes part in them. All that matters is that the story gets told, that the story repeats. Or, if you prefer to think of it like this: stories are a parasitical life form, warping lives in the service only of the story itself.


He later repeated most of this passage in a quite serious lecture that he was invited to give before the vererable Folklore Society in London in 1999, adding only a slight caveat to appease the hard-headed in his audience: "it's probably more sensible to say that we ourselves for some reason have the story shapes in our mind, and attempt to fit the facts of history into them."

"Probably" and "more sensible"! Do tell, Sir Terry, do tell!

comments.title: geomancy and gender identity?
comments.content:
First, a Johnny Appleseed story:

Driving home from a family function, my partner and I stopped at a rest stop in Leominster, MA on Saturday. Leominster, as us regular readers now know, was the birthplace of Johnny Appleseed. Lo and behold, this is the "Johnny Appleseed Memorial Rest Area" on I-495 westbound/soutbound, and home to the descendants of a "Rambo" variety apple tree planted in Ohio about 1840 by Johnny Appleseed himself. There were prominent signs on all the trees saying "Do not pick! Ripening is not yet complete at this time of year!" I did as the sign asked, and picked none from the trees. Nonetheless, a few of the windfall apples that hadn't started rotting yet, came home with me and are now under the humus in my garden. Life, in my experience, finds a way.

Then, prefacing the question:

In a recent Magic Monday, you offered a framework for understanding the 'genders' of the subtle bodies: that the physical realm's body is usually of one gender; that the emotional and intellectual bodies are usually of the opposing gender; and that the energy or astral body is usually of the same gender as the physical body. (In geomancy's four-bit "byte" terminologies, this looks like either Carcer or Conjunctio — which, truthfully, winds up saying quite a lot about the nature of certain kinds of men, and certain kinds of women). However, you allowed for the possibility and even the genuine reality of some people being set up quite differently — people whose three subtle bodies might be the same as their physical body, or people whose three subtle bodies might be arranged quite differently (a total of sixteen possible arrangements suggest themselves immediately).

I've thought about this quite a lot in the week or so since I encountered the question and your answer, and it's led to some useful meditations on the subject of 'gender identity' as it relates to the subtle bodies and as they relate to both the physical body and the figures of geomancy. However, I suspect that there's something rather more to this teaching that I'm not really getting a clear picture of.

This "subtle body" plus "geomantic figures" plus relatively-recent social tumult around gender identity feels like a useful path forward for talking about questions of gender identity in occult or pagan or magical frameworks — after all, it retains the physical binary 'at the bottom' while acknowledging that it's only a quarter of the overall experience.

The question: "doesn't the acknowledgment of the subtle body or bodies actually make the subjective, lived experiences of trans people a lot more real that their critics give them credit for? And sufficiently real that occultists should treat discussions of these questions as serious matters worthy of study and careful acceptance?

replies.title: Re: geomancy and gender identity?
replies.content:
First, no, you've got the bodies messed up. It's not Carcer and Conjunctio, it's Amissio and Acquisitio -- in the normal pattern the physical and astral bodies are one gender and the etheric and mental bodies are the other gender.

As for your broader question, I know a fair number of trans people, and I'm quite sure that the ones I know, at least, are perceiving something that actually exists. It may be a matter of variant polarities of their subtle bodies, it may be a matter of remembering a past life in which they were the opposite gender of their current physical body, or what have you, but it can be a real phenomenon. On the other hand, there are also -- inevitably -- people who are exploiting that real phenomenon for a variety of more or less sleazy or manipulative ends. Human beings being human beings, that's unavoidable.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch
replies.content:
Do you recall the old Zen story about the Western scholar who came to the Zen master? The scholar talked about this and that, imposing his own ideas and never letting the Zen master get a word in edgewise. Finally the Zen master served tea. He filled the scholar's cup, and kept on pouring until tea was splashing all over the floor. "The cup's already full!" the scholar said. "Yes, it is," said the Zen master. "Just as your mind is already full. How can I give you a taste of my tea when you bring me a cup that's already overflowing?"

If you want to study the Dolmen Arch system you need to study the Dolmen Arch system, not a hash made up of bits of the Dolmen Arch and bits of whatever feels right to you. A lot of beginners feel threatened by the idea that they should actually study a system as is, and try to make changes when they don't yet know enough about the system to make those changes intelligently. If they do that, they never actually learn anything, because they're just rehashing what they already think they know. If you want to taste the tea of the Dolmen Arch, empty your cup -- and do the lessons exactly as given, whether or not they make sense to you. By the time you finish the course, you'll understand why everything is the way it is.

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch
replies.content: Thank you! That is very helpful.

comments.title: Unity of focus
comments.content:
Last week's cosmic doctrine post brought into focus one of my major flaws, which it is hard to talk about but which I believe has been holding me back pretty much my adult life-I can never focus on anything. I always start something, continue with it for a few weeks or months, and then my focus slackens, I make excuses for missing a practice, or two, or three, and then...

My attempts to learn Persian are a good example of this. I've been trying to learn it out of the same workbook (at first borrowed from my university library when I was an undergrad, then brought used) for about ten years. I'll start on it, practice for a good two, three, maybe four months, and then my practices get less and less frequent until they stop. My last run of Persian practices ended a couple months ago, at which time I'd gotten up to about lesson 24 of 27 in the workbook (each lesson takes about a week to a week and a half to do, so I was a little less than a month from finishing). My dabbling in Golden Dawn magic has been similar-there have been a few two-three month periods where I've done the LBRP daily, and then I've let myself slack off. Another, related flaw is that I try to do too much at once-there was a period where I was trying to learn Persian, teach myself to play guitar, and learn Golden Dawn magic all at once...which didn't last all that long. Which leads me to:

1. I really need to find a way to break this pattern. Have you dealt with similar patterns of behavior before, and is there a course of action you could recommend?

2. How many things can one do at once? Say I wanted to work with Learning Ritual Magic and restart the Persian workbook at the same time. Do you think this is doable, or do I need to focus on one and put the other aside for a time?

replies.title: Re: Unity of focus
replies.content:
1) This is your challenge in this life. You, and only you, can decide whether you're going to overcome it, or not. You might consider journaling about it, to get a sense of what emotional patterns are behind it, but only you can go through the heart of the challenge and come out the other side -- or not.

2) Divided will is failure. Do one thing at a time.

replies.title: Re: Unity of focus
replies.content:
I thought this could be helpful, from The Mystic Will, Leland:

And here it may be observed that if beginners, before
taking up any pursuit, would calmly and deliberately
consider the virtues of Attention and Interest, and how
to acquire them, or bring them to bear on the proposed
study or work, we should hear much less of those who
had "begun German" without learning it, or who failed
in any other attempt. For there would in very truth
be few failures in life if those who undertake anything
first gave to it long and careful consideration by leading
observation into every detail, and, in fact, becoming
familiar with the idea, and not trusting to acquire interest
and perseverance in the future. Nine-tenths of the
difficulty and doubt or ill-at-easeness which beginners
experience, giving them the frightened feeling of "a cat
in a strange garret," and which often inspires them to
retreat, is due entirely to not having begun by training
the Attention or awakened an Interest in the subject.

comments.title: Mars retrograde?
comments.content:
Even my astrologically-clueless self is now aware than a Mars retrograde is on the way in a few weeks (September 9 - November 12). I learnt a few years ago to take Mercury retrograde seriously, and I've developed some simple sensible habits to deal with those times, but I'm having some trouble getting my head around what makes for a smooth Mars retrograde...

It seems like refraining from initiating any new projects that need to drive forward into new territory would be wise? Obviously don't start any wars, metaphorical ones included. Is it a good idea to refrain from expressing anger as much as possible, or would that be overreacting?

Any thoughts/advice most welcome, and thank you JMG for this amazing fireside forum!

replies.title: Re: Mars retrograde?
replies.content: Retrogrades are favorable for anything that involves turning back or turning within. In a Mars retrograde, direct that Mars energy inwards; use it to discipline and strengthen yourself. Don't pursue outward goals -- no, not even new personal weightlifting records! -- but work on who you are. Get rid of old habits that don't serve you any more. Clean out your attic, physically or metaphorically. Clear the ground so you can plunge ahead once Mars turns back around.
